case_name: Ammonium perchlorate and metolachlor control from lake water for groundwater recharge under moderate cost and limited-operator constraints
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for lake water containing perchlorate and metolachlor intended for groundwater recharge while balancing multi-contaminant removal, moderate cost limits, and limited operator skill.

input_data.scenario_background: A water utility plans to divert impacted lake water to a managed aquifer recharge basin during dry months. Monitoring shows contamination linked to upstream runoff and legacy handling of ammonium perchlorate, creating a dual-target problem because the recharge program must control both perchlorate and the herbicide metolachlor before infiltration. The utility has moderate capital available but limited operator attention, so the preferred solution must balance reliable dual-contaminant performance against operational simplicity, residuals burden, and compliance stability during seasonal water-quality shifts.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Treated water should achieve stable reduction of perchlorate and metolachlor to levels suitable for protective groundwater recharge operation and minimize risk of contaminant migration to the receiving aquifer.
secondary_goals: maintain robust performance during seasonal increases in natural organic matter and turbidity
avoid high-skill process control where possible
provide practical pretreatment for recharge reliability

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: prefer to avoid large brine or concentrate streams and keep residuals handling manageable for a municipal utility
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: process should tolerate seasonal lake-water variation in turbidity and DOC without frequent operator intervention

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce turbidity and natural organic matter loading from lake water
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish suspended solids and protect downstream adsorptive units
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: remove metolachlor and lower organic competition
core_unit_functions.selective_perchlorate_ion_exchange: selectively remove perchlorate to low residual concentrations

applicability_rationale: the contaminant pair favors a split-duty train because metolachlor is well suited to adsorption while perchlorate is more reliably addressed by selective ion exchange
pretreatment is important for lake water with moderate turbidity and DOC to stabilize downstream media performance
placing GAC ahead of perchlorate ion exchange helps reduce organic fouling pressure and improves compliance reliability
this route balances strong technical performance with lower operator burden than membrane or biologically intensive alternatives
the process avoids the very high concentrate-management burden associated with high-recovery membrane treatment
potential_risks: spent GAC replacement or reactivation logistics are required
selective resin replacement or regeneration planning is needed
higher sulfate or nitrate swings can affect ion exchange run length depending on resin choice and operating conditions
seasonal DOC spikes may shorten GAC bed life if pretreatment performance degrades

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove particulate matter and reduce organic loading
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: provide solids polishing and stable feed to biologically active media
core_unit_functions.biological_activated_carbon: adsorb metolachlor and support biologically mediated perchlorate reduction under controlled conditions

applicability_rationale: BAC can offer a compact combined route for organic micropollutant control and perchlorate reduction when biological activity is successfully maintained
this option can reduce chemical regenerant demand and minimize brine residuals compared with ion exchange or membranes
for recharge applications, biologically based treatment can be attractive if a utility can support process monitoring and stable operating conditions
the route is plausible but ranks below the leading option because the utility has limited operator skill and the biological component requires more careful control
potential_risks: perchlorate bioreduction performance can be sensitive to redox management and electron donor strategy
operator oversight is needed to avoid unstable biological conditions
metolachlor removal may depend on adsorption capacity and biodegradation kinetics rather than a single predictable barrier
startup and seasonal recovery can be slower than purely adsorptive systems

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: remove bulk suspended solids and some organic matter
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon: remove metolachlor through batch adsorption before solids separation

applicability_rationale: PAC is technically capable for metolachlor control and may be attractive where GAC contactors are not preferred
selective ion exchange remains a strong perchlorate barrier
this route can work for variable lake-water events because PAC dose can be adjusted during contaminant peaks
it ranks below the top option because slurry handling, chemical feed control, and solids management add day-to-day complexity for limited staff
potential_risks: PAC dosing optimization is needed to maintain metolachlor removal during DOC fluctuations
additional sludge production increases solids handling burden
under-dosing or poor mixing can cause variable micropollutant control
ion exchange media life still depends on source-water chemistry and pretreatment consistency

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce fouling load from raw lake water
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: protect membrane system from suspended solids
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_reverse_osmosis: provide strong rejection of metolachlor and perchlorate
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust treated water chemistry before recharge

applicability_rationale: membrane treatment is technically strong for simultaneous control of inorganic and organic contaminants
this route may be selected if the project also needs broad salt and trace contaminant reduction beyond the two primary targets
despite strong removal capability, it ranks lower because moderate cost limits, operator-skill limitations, and concentrate handling constraints are misaligned with high-pressure membranes
for groundwater recharge, concentrate disposal can become a major permitting and lifecycle challenge
potential_risks: concentrate management is difficult and potentially costly
membrane fouling risk increases with seasonal lake-water turbidity and DOC variability
higher instrumentation and operator attention are needed
post-treatment stabilization and integrity monitoring add complexity

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_sedimentation: reduce particulate and some organic loading
core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process: oxidize metolachlor and other oxidizable organic micropollutants
core_unit_functions.biological_filtration: remove oxidation byproducts and provide polishing

applicability_rationale: AOP can address metolachlor and similar organic micropollutants in surface waters
the route is included as a technically partial option because it does not directly provide a robust primary barrier for perchlorate
it ranks last because the case requires reliable dual-contaminant treatment, and adding a separate perchlorate-specific barrier would further increase complexity and cost
potential_risks: perchlorate is generally not effectively removed by conventional AOP as a standalone solution
oxidation byproduct formation and quenching demand increase with DOC
UV or oxidant systems require more technical oversight than preferred for this utility
without a dedicated perchlorate step, compliance reliability is poor

preferred_rank_pattern: combined trains that separate metolachlor control from perchlorate control should rank near the top
membrane treatment should appear as technically strong but not first because of concentrate, cost, and operator constraints
options lacking a dedicated reliable perchlorate barrier should rank low

must_reflect: need to handle two contaminants with different treatment affinities
moderate municipal budget rather than premium advanced treatment budget
limited operator skill and desire to avoid highly intervention-intensive processes
importance of manageable residuals for a recharge project

must_contain: why metolachlor is compatible with activated carbon based treatment
why perchlorate typically favors selective ion exchange or controlled biological reduction rather than oxidation alone
why lake-water turbidity and DOC justify pretreatment ahead of adsorptive or membrane steps
why membrane options are downgraded despite broad contaminant removal capability

should_mention: seasonal DOC and turbidity impacts on media run length or fouling
resin exhaustion or competition effects from background anions
biological process stability and monitoring needs
